content.problemStatement: Given a selectionSort function that accepts an array as a parameter, sort the array using the **Selection sort** algorithm.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N (the size of the array). The second line contains N space‑separated integers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the array sorted in non‑decreasing order on a single line. Numbers are separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The array is repeatedly scanned to find the smallest remaining element and swapped to the front, producing a sorted sequence.
Each pass moves the smallest unsorted element to its correct position, turning the descending order into ascending order.
The input is already sorted; selection sort performs swaps that leave the array unchanged.

solution.approach: We implement the classic selection sort algorithm. For each position i from 0 to n‑2 we find the smallest element in the suffix i..n‑1, then swap it with the element at i. After the i‑th iteration the prefix 0..i is sorted and will never be touched again. The algorithm uses only a constant amount of extra memory and works in‑place. For each test case we read the array, apply selection sort, and print the sorted array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case because for each of the N positions we scan the remaining unsorted part. The total time is O(T·N^2).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input array.
